text: now these days it's practically become
the norm for modern-day sequels to erase
undermine or otherwise destroy the
achievements of their predecessors
undoing years of hard work and fan
Goodwill with a bunch of idiotic
decisions that are so bizarre it's hard
to even understand the thought process
behind them but back in the early '90s
stuff like that was still pretty rare
that is until Alien 3 chest bursted its
way onto the scene or alien cubed I
don't know I've never really understood
the type face for that one anyway anyway
the point is that the third entry in the
alien franchise has become pretty
Infamous and controversial for all the
creative decisions that were made
although considering the nightmares that
awaited this franchise in the decades
ahead this thing is practically Citizen
Cane by
comparison I'll do the
fingering but the story behind the
making of This film is even more
controversial and dramatic than the
movie itself a story of corporate greed
endless interference creative Deadlocks
and disputes so extreme that they caused
the director to publicly disown his own
movie in short Alien 3 truly was
production hell so let's learn about it
and embark on this journey together
shall we the success of James Cameron's
aliens convinced Fox that there was
still plenty of life left in the alien
franchise and more importantly plenty of
money still to be made by 1987 it was
unanimously agreed that a third alien
movie needed to be made and preferably
soon to capitalize on the momentum of
the second movie The only fly in the
ointment here were Brandy Wine
Productions the company who actually
owned the screen rights to the alien
franchise they were Keen to avoid just
doing another conventional Sequel and
wanted to broaden the scope of the whole
alien Universe with something bigger and
much more ambitious their plan was to
film two movies back to back featuring
an epic confrontation between the way
and utani Corporation and a faction of
space Communists who want to weaponize
the aliens against them and if you're
able to spot the subtle allegories for
the actual cold war that was going on in
the real world then congratulations
Captain Obvious here's your degree in
media literacy so you can go on to
become a pretentious arsole YouTuber
making 10-hour long video essays about
the philosophical undertones of the
Powerpuff
Girls anyway the first movie would focus
more on the character of hicks with ripl
relegated to a minor Cameo appearance
and believe it or not this actually
suited Sigourney we who was still mad at
the studio for cutting out major
portions of Ripley's backstory in Aliens
and was becoming increasingly tired of
the role and worried about typ casting
the intention was to have Ridley Scott
direct both movies but he was busy
working on black rain at the time and so
wasn't available instead renie Harlen
was approached to direct and cyberpunk
author William Gibson was hired to write
the scripts unfortunately the studio
were unhappy with the finished product
feeling like it was too conventional and
action heavy and when Gibson was asked
to rewrite it he decided to leave the
project alog together ironically enough
though his original script has gone on
to develop a strong following online and
has even been turned into an audio book
with many people considering it superior
to the finished movie ah what might have
been with Gibson out of the way renie
Harland suggested cult horror writer
Eric red as a replacement red struggled
to come up with a new concept and
frustrated by constant Studio Medlin he
also bailed on the project after a
couple of months later describing it as
the one script I completely own because
it wasn't my script it was the rushed
product of too many story conferences an
interference with no time to write and
it turned out o are
crap David toi was next in line to take
a crack at it but unfortunately for him
real world events had overtaken the
projects the fall of the Soviet Union
made the Cold War in space concept
obsolete so twah instead decided to set
his script on a prison planet where the
aliens were being used in biological
warfare experiments the studio were also
getting cold feet on doing an alien
movie without Ripley and decided that
she needed to be front and center again
which led to a pretty tense call with
Sigourney Weaver who finally agreed to
come back on the condition she got paid
$4 million which was actually a pretty
hefty amount back then and that the
script wouldn't have any guns in it
that's great that's just [ __ ] great
man Jesus way to suck the fun out of
things Sigourney by this point renie
harlon was getting bored and pissed off
with the constant changes and delays and
walked out of the project leading the
studio to replace him with new Zealand
director Vincent Wards Wards new idea
was for Ripley's Escape pod to crash
land on a prison Monastery planet made
entirely out of
woods not entirely sure how he imagined
that one working out but whatever the
movie would have had heavy religious
overtones and have been much more
character driven focusing instead on
Ripley and her relationship with the
alien the studio were either impressed
enough or desperate enough to Greenlight
his idea and pretty soon Ward got to
work unfortunately the boys Brandy Wine
were increasingly uneasy about Ward's
plan and their pessimism soon spread to
the fox Executives as well with John
landow describing it as more on the
artsy fartsy side than on the big
commercial side Ward was summoned to
their head offices and given a list of
Demands to rewrite the script which he
promptly rejected and funnily enough he
was fired soon after by now it was 1990
they'd been slogging away at this
[ __ ] thing for 3 years and they had
nothing to show for it but a trail of
aborted scripts Running Out of Time the
executive at Brandy Wine ended up
rewriting the script themselves by
merging together elements of twah and
Wards pitches into something that mostly
resembled the final products it was
still unfinished at the time and nobody
was especially happy with it but it was
the best they had and so there was no
choice but to just go with it and hope
for the best not a great plan David
finer who at the time was best known for
directing music videos was approached to
direct he was already a massive fan of
the alien franchise and well you can't
exactly blame the guy for signing on but
it would be a decision that would end up
haunting him for the rest of his career
production finally got underway at
Pinewood Studios in January 1991 and
pretty soon any enthusiasm that Fincher
might have had for the project
evaporated once it became clear the
studio were going to be heavily involved
unwilling to trust such an inexperienced
director with a $60 million movie they
instead tried to micromanage every
aspect of the production leading to
constant clashes and an increasingly
toxic atmosphere on set Fincher's
original vision for the film was
gradually diluted and cut by an
interference Studio that couldn't seem
to quite decide what they wanted as they
became increasingly fixated on the
budget Fox began panicking and making
cuts the shooting schedule was reduced
from 93 to just 70 days and the number
of special effects shots were cut in
half the tighter schedule meant cast and
crew were forced to work much longer
hours six days a week often having to
cut Corners to make their deadlines and
inevitably accidents were bound to
happen at one point a pyrot technic
device backfired during a big action
scene causing severe burns for several
people in the vicinity and shutting down
production while one crew member was
rushed to hospital all wasn't exactly
going well on the effect side of things
either Fincher wanted this version of
the alien to be much more fast and agile
than the men in rubber suits we'd seen
in the previous movies but since CGI
wasn't up to the task at the time they
had to find another solution and I'm not
going to lie some of the [ __ ] they tried
was just bashit insane like gluing a
bunch of rubber alien Prosthetics to a
[ __ ] dog and having it run around the
set needless to say the results were
suboptimal at best I mean just look at
this thing it's almost as bad as the
original Predator suit finally though
the team came up with an unusual
combination of miniature puppets mounted
on rods and cables manipulated by
several crew members that was shot doing
various actions and then composited into
liveaction footage to produce the final
sequences I mean it was definitely fast
and agile that's for sure but it also
looks like complete dog [ __ ] it's
horribly fake and jerky and dated in a
way that the other movies never were and
in some ways I think it's actually a
perfect metaphor for what Alien 3 was a
cheap nasty and tacky addition to a once
great franchise that's aged way worse
than its predecessor who came out more
than a decade earlier Alien 3 finally
released in May 1992 to mixed reviews
going on to make about $160 million
worldwide against a 60 million budget
considered by most people to be kind of
a disappointment the decision to kill
off all the major characters from the
previous movie also prompted a big
backlash and I'm not going to lie to
this very day it still leaves a sour
taste in my mouth David Fincher himself
was so disillusioned and disgusted by
the experience that he publicly disowned
the film and refused to have any further
participation even refusing to help out
when Fox decided to put together a
director's cut for home media the
assembly cut as it became known restored
about 30 minutes of cut footage and
helped to fix a few of the major plot
holes but ultimately even that couldn't
change the fact that Alien 3 is a
relentlessly Grim Bleak mean-spirited
and Visually depressing movie that I
never have the slightest inclination to
rewatch ultimately I think Sigourney
Weaver herself best summed up the
experience of making this film when she
spoke about it in 2006 what was ironic
was that fox chose David Fincher who was
so talented and from the second he got
on the job they undermined him by not
giving him what he asked for for me it
was a real education on how not to make
a
movie anyway that's all I've got for
today go away now here
